coredata.dc:title: A facile method to prepare fluorescent carbon dots and their application in selective colorimetric sensing of silver ion through the formation of silver nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Ayaz Ahmed, Khan Behlol
P, Suresh Kumar
Veerappan, Anbazhagan
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a laboratory convenient method for the preparation of blue color emitting fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) in 60min by boiling the alkaline solution of pectin. The C-dots derived from pectin detects selectively silver ion by forming silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) without any irradiation or heating or additional reducing agents. As prepared AgNPs appears yellow in color and showed the characteristic surface plasmon resonance maximum at 410nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed crystalline, spherical AgNPs with size range from 10–15nm. Cyclic voltammetry study revealed that the lower reduction potential of C-dots than that of silver ion favors the reduction of Ag+ to Ag°. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy showed the charge transfer value for the redox reaction of C-dots as 200Ωcm2. In the presence of Ag+, C-dots fluorescence emission was turned from blue to cyan to green to colorless, accompanying the quenching and red shift in emission maximum at 450nm. Interference study clearly showed that the C-dots have high preference for Ag+ ion than the other interfering metal ions. The proposed sensor system selectively senses Ag+ ion in water at micromolar concentration and also offers an easy procedure to prepare AgNPs in the presence of other interfering metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
Silver ion sensor
Silver nanoparticles
Cyclic voltammetry
Pectin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic preparation of C-dots from pectin.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots prepared from alkaline pectin solution at room temperature (black line) and heating (blue line), excitation–360nm, (B) Time dependent changes in the fluorescence maximum at 450nm and (C) TEM image of C-dots prepared by boiling alkaline pectin solution.

(A) 1H NMR spectra of pectin. Inset corresponds to expanded spectra for the region of 3.0–5.2ppm,, (B) 1H NMR spectra of C-dots obtained by boiling alkaline pectin solution, (C) FTIR spectra of as-prepared C-dots and (D) proposed mechanism for the formation of C-dots from pectin.

Colorimetric silver ion sensor. (A) C-dots photographs taken in the presence of 25µM of various metal ions in room light. (B) Absorption spectra of C-dots aqueous solution with 25µM of metal ions. (C) Interference study. Black bars represent the absorbance of C-dots at 410nm in the presence of various metal ions (25µM) and red bars represent the absorbance of C-dots containing 25µM of Ag+ ion and the background metal ions (25µM).
(A) and (B) TEM image of as-prepared AgNPs. Inset corresponds to the SAED pattern of AgNPs.

Fluorimetric silver ion sensor. (A) C-dots photographs taken under UV light in the presence of 25µM of various metal ions, excitation wavelength–360nm. (B) The Fluorimetric response of C-dots to various metal ions (25µM), excitation wavelength–360nm; emission wavelength–450nm. (C) Change of C-dots fluorescence vs Ag+ concentration. Inset corresponds to C-dots fluorescence change at 450nm vs Ag+ concentration.
(A) Cyclic voltammetry and (B) electrochemical impedance spectroscopy data of C-dots in aqueous solution at pH 9.

head.ce:title: A facile method to prepare fluorescent carbon dots and their application in selective colorimetric sensing of silver ion through the formation of silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemical and Biotechnology, SASTRA University, Thanjavur 613401, Tamil Nadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Chemical and Biotechnology, SASTRA University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a laboratory convenient method for the preparation of blue color emitting fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) in 60min by boiling the alkaline solution of pectin. The C-dots derived from pectin detects selectively silver ion by forming silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) without any irradiation or heating or additional reducing agents. As prepared AgNPs appears yellow in color and showed the characteristic surface plasmon resonance maximum at 410nm. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed crystalline, spherical AgNPs with size range from 10–15nm. Cyclic voltammetry study revealed that the lower reduction potential of C-dots than that of silver ion favors the reduction of Ag+ to Ag°. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy showed the charge transfer value for the redox reaction of C-dots as 200Ωcm2. In the presence of Ag+, C-dots fluorescence emission was turned from blue to cyan to green to colorless, accompanying the quenching and red shift in emission maximum at 450nm. Interference study clearly showed that the C-dots have high preference for Ag+ ion than the other interfering metal ions. The proposed sensor system selectively senses Ag+ ion in water at micromolar concentration and also offers an easy procedure to prepare AgNPs in the presence of other interfering metal ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) have recently received significant scientific attention owing to their interesting physicochemical and optoelectronic properties including photostability, photoluminescence and electron transfer behavior [1–3]. The tuneable photoluminescence properties have found useful in vitro and in vivo bioimaging [4–6]. The electron donor and electron acceptor potential of C-dots have found successful in sensing and photocatalytic application [7–10]. Moreover, C-dots are biocompatible and less toxic than the traditional semiconductor quantum dots [11,12]. Therefore, from the time of discovery by Xu et al., several new applications are emerging for C-dots [13]. This leads to the development of several synthetic techniques including laser ablation, electrochemical synthesis, arc discharge, hydrothermal and microwave heating, etc [14–17]. Most of these methods are either required complex post-treatment or required sophisticated instrumentation. There are very few reports exist on the synthesis of C-dots by simple techniques, such as dehydration of carbohydrates by strong acids, alkaline treatment of cetylpyridinium chloride monohydrate and oxidation of capsicum extract [18–23]. Of late, there is considerable progress in the synthesis of C-dots through green chemistry method utilizing natural bio-resource, such as orange peel [17], soya milk [24], cow milk [25] and apple juice [26]. Despite the advantages, the use of renewable bio-precursors suffers from the uncertainty about the chemical components responsible for the formation of C-dots. Therefore, a more defined, more robust, cost-effective and eco-friendly approach is still warranted for large scale preparation.

Pectin is a natural carbohydrate polymer present in the pomace of citrus fruits. It is composed of a linear backbone of (1-4) linked α-D-galacturonic acid residue with varying degree of methylesterified carboxyl groups. It is widely used as gelling, thickening and stabilizing agent in the food industry [27,28]. In this paper, we described a laboratory convenient method to synthesize blue fluorescence C-dots by simply heating the alkaline solution of pectin in a round bottom flask. The proposed method to obtain blue color emitting C-dots is quite different from other protocols where the formation of pectin hydrogel and hydrothermal treatment is a prerequisite for the preparation of C-dots [29]. The formation of C-dots and their fluorescent behaviour under various conditions were investigated. A possible mechanism for the formation of the C-dots from pectin was proposed from the collective information obtained from the literature reports on the carbonization of carbohydrates [30–34] and also by the help of FTIR and 1H NMR analysis.

Of late there has been great interest in exploiting the fluorescence C-dots for selective and sensitive detection of heavy metal ion, sulfide ion, glutathione, tetracyclines [35–40]. Herein, we report that the C-dots derived from pectin detect silver ion through the formation of yellow color silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). As prepared AgNPs was characterized by UV–visible, TEM and zeta potential analyzer. For the first time, the reduction potential of C-dots was estimated by cyclic voltammetry. The estimated negative reduction potential of C-dots than that of silver ion favours the sensing through electron transfer reaction. Moreover, the proposed sensing system offers an eco-friendly method to obtain stable AgNPs without any reducing agent or irradiation or heating.

Sodium borohydride and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Merck, India. Silver nitrate was purchased from Fisher Scientific, India. Pectin was obtained from Alfa Aesar. All the glassware were cleaned with freshly prepared aqua regia (3:1, HCl:HNO3) and rinsed thoroughly with water. Then they were dry sterilized using hot air oven at 160°C for 3h, prior to use.

For the preparation of C-dots, pectin from citrus peel (Alfa Aesar, India) was used and characterized by 1H-NMR (Burker 300MHz) and the pectin degree of esterification was determined by the titration method (see supporting information). Typically, alkaline pectin solution was prepared by dissolving 100mg pectin in 10mL of 100mM NaOH solution and stirred for about 30min and then brought down to room temperature. This mixture is transferred carefully to a round bottom flask and heated in the laboratory heating mantle for 60min. The appearance of the dark brown color indicates the formation of C-dots. The formation of fluorescent C-dots was confirmed by observing blue color fluorescent while irradiating at 360nm using UV–vis lamp. The excess unreacted NaOH was removed by dialysis (dialysis bag cut off 1000Da) against double distilled water for 24h.

C-dots fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Jasco-FP8200 spectrofluorimeter. The spectral slit width was set at 2.5nm for both excitation and emission monochromators. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was performed on Thermo Scientific Evolution 201 spectrophotometer operated at 1nm resolution. The size and morphology were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL-JEM 1011, Japan) operating at an accelerated voltage of 200kV. The samples were prepared by placing a drop of NP solution on a graphite grid and drying it in vacuum. The dried powders of C-dots were subjected to FTIR spectroscopy measurements. These measurements were carried out on a Perkin-Elmer spectrum-one instrument in the diffuse reflectance mode at a resolution of 1cm−1 in KBr pellet. 1H NMR was measured in a Bruker 300MHz instrument.

C-dots prepared with 100mM NaOH was used for the sensor studies. Metal ion for sensor studies was prepared by mixing the requisite amount of salt in double distilled water. Typically, 200µL of C-dots was added to 9.8mL of water containing different metal ions and monitored the color change. The assays and the changes in absorption and fluorescence spectra were performed and monitored at room temperature. The photographs were taken with a digital camera after 10min of mixing.

All electrochemical studies were carried out in aqueous medium using a platinum disc working electrode, platinum wire counter electrode and saturated calomel electrode as reference electrode (CHI660E electrochemical workstation; CH Instruments, USA). Electrochemical impedance studies were carried out over a frequency range of 1Hz to 1MHz with an ac amplitude of 5mV and the data were fitted with modified Randle׳s circuit for a diffusion controlled process (R(C(RW)) using ZSimpWin software (EG & G).

The preparation of C-dots using biopolymer, pectin was illustrated in Fig. 1A. Initial experiments were carried at room temperature by mixing 10mL of aqueous suspension of pectin (100mg) with 100mM of NaOH. The color changes from colorless to light yellow indicating the carbonization of pectin. It has been noted that the color change is accompanied by the formation of precipitates in the bottom of the test tube. These solutions produced greenish blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (Fig. 1). Considering the non-fluorescent nature of the starting material, the obtained fluorescence can be attributed to the presence of C-dots in the solution. Attempts were also made to synthesis C-dots at room temperature with varying concentration of NaOH. Every time, the C-dots preparations end up with the formation of precipitates at the bottom, moreover, these solutions showed very weak fluorescence (Fig. S1A and C).

To obtain highly fluorescent C-dots, we boiled (100°C) the alkaline solution of pectin in a laboratory heating mantle. Heating the alkaline pectin solution in a laboratory heating mantle proceed with a color change from light yellow to brownish yellow in 60min, indicating complete carbonization (Fig. 1 and Fig. S1B). As-prepared C-dots showed strong blue fluorescent under UV irradiation (Fig. 1). Fig. S1 shows that the fluorescence intensity increases with the concentration of NaOH and reaches a maximum at 100mM of NaOH. Further increases in the NaOH concentration caused a reduction in the fluorescence intensity. The quantum yield of the aqueous solution of C-dots was determined using quinine sulfate as the reference and was found to be 5.38 at 100mM NaOH (Fig. S1D). This value is comparable to the literature reported values for non-passivating carbon dots [33,41–43]. These results suggest that the surface of C-dots was probably damaged at the high concentration of NaOH and 100mM NaOH is sufficient to produce photoluminescence C-dots with high quantum yield. Noteworthy that after heating there is no visible precipitates, indicating that most of the pectin was carbonized. Fig. S2 shows the variation in the fluorescence intensity and emission maximum with respect to the excitation wavelength, indicating that the maximum fluorescent intensity for C-dots was obtained at an excitation wavelength of 360nm. Fluorescence spectra of C-dots prepared at room temperature and heating was shown in Fig. 2A. When excited at 360nm, the C-dots prepared with 100mM NaOH at room temperature showed very weak fluorescence emission peak at 495nm. Whereas, C-dots prepared by boiling the alkaline pectin solution exhibits a strong fluorescence (150 fold) with peak maximum at 450nm (Fig. 2A and Fig. S1D). Time dependent studies showed that the half time required to carbonization of pectin was 15.64min and the carbonization was almost completed in 60min, after that the fluorescence intensity does not vary significantly (Fig. 2B and Fig. S3).

The formation of C-dots was confirmed from TEM micrographs. Fig. 2C clearly shows that the C-dots are well dispersed and spherical in shape with an average size of 4nm (Fig. 2C). The formation of C-dots from the carbon precursor, pectin was investigated by 1H NMR and FTIR spectroscopy ( Fig. 3). The 1H NMR spectra of pectin shows peak between 3.6 and 4.6ppm (Fig. 3A), which can be attributed to the protons attached with –OH, –CH2–O–CH2–, –CH2=CH2–and–HC=O. 1H NMR spectrum of as-prepared C-dots has a similar pattern in aliphatic region as that of pectin (Fig. 3B) with an additional peaks between 1 and 2.2ppm, indicating the presence of protons attached to methylene and methyl moieties. In addition, it shows a new proton peak at 8.3ppm indicating the aldehydic proton. Also in 13C NMR, a peak for –HC=O was observed between 175 and 190ppm (Fig. S4). The peak at 98.92ppm can be attributed to –C=C– attached to a polar group and the peak between 65 and 80ppm is assigned to –C–OH and –C–O–C– groups (Fig. S4). FTIR analysis of C-dots showed peaks at 3425, 2925, 2857, 1615 and 1028cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching of O–H, C–H (assym), C–H (sym), C=C and C–O–C groups, respectively and a peak at 1410cm−1 is attributed to the bending of O–H groups (Fig. 3C). These results suggest that the surfaces of the C-dots are sufficiently self passivated with –OH, –C–O–C– groups while heating, which is responsible for excellent water solubility and strong fluorescence.

Based on the FTIR and 1H NMR and also based on the literature reports on the carbonization of carbohydrates [30–34], we propose that pectin on treatment with NaOH undergoes hydrolysis to form majorly galactose, a reducing sugar and galacturonic acid, a non-reducing sugar [44]. The dehydration and decomposition of galactose takes place in the presence of galacturonic acid and can generates soluble compounds like furfural, ketones and several organic acids like acetic, levulinic, formic acid etc [30–34]. These products undergo polymerization and condensation to yield soluble polymers. The formed polymer can aromatizes and form aromatic clusters via aldol condensation and cycloaddition reactions [30]. These clusters presumably undergo nuclear burst at a critical supersaturation concentration to produce luminescent C-dots (Fig. 3D).

The stability of the C-dots was followed by measuring the fluorescence intensity at 450nm over a period of 2 months and no significant change in the intensity was observed. The fluorescence peak position at 450nm remains unaltered at any pH, ranging from 3 to 9, indicates that the as-prepared C-dots are highly stable in a wide pH range (Fig. S5) and can be stored as lyophilized powder for a longer period. Therefore, in the subsequent studies, we used the C-dots prepared by the heating method.

Time-resolved fluorescence of C-dots showed two-different life-times for the excited C-dots, τ 1 (3.01ns) and τ 2 (0.70ns) (Fig. S6) and indicating that the prepared C-dots can act as donor or acceptor and could have the ability to transfer electrons towards the metal ion [45]. Hence, we explored the newly prepared C-dots for sensing a series of metal ion such as Mg2+. Ca2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Na+, K+ and Fe3+, Au3+ in aqueous solution. In a typical experiment, 200µL of C-dots were mixed with 10mL of 25µM heavy metal ion and allowed to stand for 2min at room temperature. Addition of 25μM of Ag+ ion selectively changes the C-dots color to yellow color, suggesting that C-dots senses Ag+ ion through the formation of silver nanoparticles. The formation of AgNPs in C-dots solution was confirmed by absorption spectroscopy. It shows the characteristic surface plasmon resonance maximum of AgNPs at 410nm ( Fig. 4B). Absorption spectra of C-dots with other metal ion did not show any special spectral features. A control experiment has been performed by incubating the silver nitrate with pectin alone. No visible color change even after incubated for 24h incubation, indicating the prerequisite of C-dots for sensing Ag+ ion. The presence of other metal ion in C-dots had little influence on the selective colorimetric sensing of Ag+ that confirmed the high selectivity (Fig. 4C). The colorimetric detection limit of Ag+ was evaluated by adding different concentration of Ag+ ion to C-dots. The concentration dependent studies showed the characteristic SPR peak of AgNPs was detected with the addition of 7μM of AgNO3, after that it showed concentration dependent increase in the absorbance intensity (Fig. S7).

TEM images revealed spherical AgNPs with size ranging from 10 to 15nm ( Fig. 5). Detectable lattice fringes in the TEM images and rings in the selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern revealed the crystalline structure of AgNPs (inset of Fig. 5B). Zeta potential estimated for AgNPs was −34.2mV, indicating that the NPs are well dispersed in the aqueous solution due to electro-static repulsion between the adjacent particles (Fig. S8). C-dots mediated formation of AgNPs reported here is a straight forward method and quite different from other protocols where external heating or photoirradiation or additional stabilizing agents is a prerequisite for reduction and stabilization of the nanoparticles [46–48]. Noteworthy, the AgNPs remain stable for more than 2 months without an additional stabilizing agent, indicating that the C-dots serve as a scaffold and direct the formation of stable NPs. It is worth mentioning that C-dots could produce a stable colloidal solution of AgNPs between pH 6 and 10 and not suitable to produce AgNPs below pH 6 (Fig. S9).

Next, we investigated the effect of different metal ion on the C-dots fluorescence properties ( Fig. 6). Ag+ was the only ion that resulted in significant fluorescence quenching, demonstrating the fluorimetric sensing of C-dots. Interestingly, addition of Ag+ produces concentration dependent change in C-dots fluorescence from blue to cyan to green to colorless, indicating the change in the electronic state of the C-dots [Fig. S10A]. Fluorescence spectroscopy study supports this observation by recording a red shift in C-dots fluorescence with concominant quenching [Fig. 6C]. To evaluate the selectivity of the sensor, we investigated the influence of interfering ion such as Mg2+. Ca2+, Pb2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Na+, K+ and Fe3+, Au3+ on the fluorescence behaviour of the C-dots. The fluorescence intensity of solutions containing background metal ion showed no obvious variation compared with those containing Ag+ ion (Fig. S10B). These results suggest that these metal ions appear does not interfere with the fluorimetric determination of Ag+ ion.

As described above, C-dots sense Ag+ by reducing it to AgNPs. To understand the reduction mechanism of C-dots, cyclic voltammetry of C-dots were carried out in aqueous medium at two different pH conditions, viz. 3 and 9 using a Pt disc working electrode and Pt counter electrode at a scan rate of 20mV/sec. E° for C-dots were obtained to be ca. −0.2V vs. SCE at pH 9 ( Fig. 7), which indicates its strong reduction capability towards Ag+ to Ag°. On the other hand, well-defined redox peaks were not observed when the electrolyte was at pH 3 (Fig. S11), which further support our observations that formation of AgNPs is mostly favored in alkaline solutions. Furthermore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out at a DC potential of −0.2V and ac amplitude of 5mV between 1Hz and 100kHz. Diffusion-controlled reaction was observed for C-dots at pH 9, while more complicated response was observed for pH 3. From the EIS data, charge-transfer value of the redox reaction of C-dots was estimated to be about 200Ω.cm2 (Fig. 7B), supporting our earlier observations on its reducing properties. The mechanism of silver ion sensing can be understand based on the difference in the standard reduction potential of C-dots (E°=−0.2V) and Ag+ (E°=+0. 80V). According to electrochemical series, a metal with highest E° has the greatest tendency to get reduced. The Gibbs energy for the reduction of Ag+ can be evaluated using the standard reduction potential from electrochemical series as shown below:
(1) Ag++C-dots→Ag°

G=Gibbs energy, n=valence, F=Faraday constant and E°cell=standard potential difference. The resulting, large negative Gibbs energy shows that the Ag+ reduction as shown in Eq. 1 is favoured reduction reaction.

In summary, we demonstrated the facile method to synthesis highly fluorescent C-dots using a naturally occurring carbohydrate polymer, pectin. UV–visible, fluorescence, TEM, FTIR and 1H NMR measurements were used to characterize the resulting C-dots. Our results indicated that C-dots have an ability to selectively sense Ag+ at the concentration of micromolar. Cyclic voltammetry study confirms that the C-dots have the potential to reduce Ag+ to Ag0. As formed AgNPs was characterized by UV–visible, TEM and Zeta potential analyzer. This method shows high selectivity for Ag+ ion over the metal ions with a colorimetric detection limit of 7μM. The easy preparation and high stability of the C-dots allow this method to be very simple and easy to implement in the synthesis of stable AgNPs. In summary, our study represents a step forward in the development of non-toxic sensing system for detecting heavy metal ions in aqueous solution.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: KBAA earnestly acknowledge the teaching assistantship from SASTRA University. PSK (SB/FT/CS/−148/2013) and VA (SB/FT/LS-217/2012) acknowledge the financial support from Department of Science and Technology, Government of India, India. We thank central research facility (R&M/0021/SCBT-007/2012-13), SASTRA University for the infrastructure. We thank Dr. Venkatesan and Mr. Ganavel, Pondicherry University for their help in time resolved fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2016.04.053.
